turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And that gas when it goes over the Cities Services by ordinary customer channeled going in and outside of Kansas, is that correct too?

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: After Cities Service purchases the gas.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But at that transaction, right there at the wellhead is what Cities Services is here about and what Kansas says you can't get -- put on it any other phase of transaction or monetary relationship except if you want it, you've got to pay at least 11 cents.

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: If Kansas does not tell that to Cities Service. Kansas tells Magnolia.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Tells Magnolia.

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That you can't sell it the Cities Service less than 11 cents, is that right?

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: If that's all you're going to do with it is sell it to Cities Service for 11 -- for less than 11 cents you can't produce --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But in fact, it sells it to a person who as a matter of vast range of business as interstate outlet for the gas he's getting, is that correct too?

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: If it sells it to the Cities Service, it sells it to such a person. That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Suppose Magnolia buy or sells it for less than that, what can it be done to -- under Kansas law --

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: Well it --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- if it's a valid law.

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: The order says that they shall not take it out. So it would be --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What sanction (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: -- from taking gas.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What sanction does your law provide for one who violates an order of your Commission?

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: Well, I -- there are some single provisions I suppose --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: It's a criminal provision (Inaudible) --

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: For violation of an order?

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: Well, the way the Kansas Commission has entered in this case is by so cost.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Does the -- is it a violation of your criminal statute?

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: No, no, I'm sure that's true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: That's -- let's say Corporation Commission of Kansas. The obvious method of enforcing this order was to -- would be to prevent them from taking the gas out of the field.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You could enjoin it from violating this order, couldn't you?

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And what -- are you really -- the colloquy between you and me get down to this that Kansas has done nothing to Cities Service, doesn't know Cities Service, doesn't care about Cities Service, but Magnolia -- and Magnolia is free not to tell to anybody to oblige, is that right? As far as this order is concerned, Magnolia didn't sell to anybody, is that right?

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: As far as the order is concerned --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: -- that's -- that's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Magnolia -- but if it sell, it sell -- it must sell at 11 -- for 11 cents or attribute 11 cents, is that right?

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: Yes, that's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And Kansas doesn't know the existence of Cities Service, is that right? Therefore, Cities Service is not involved. Magnolia is free as a bird. Therefore, nothing has happened.

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: Well, I -- I think you over simplify and overstate my position there. I want to point out that this order was made to correct the continued monopolistic practices of the few pipeline companies out in the Kansas Hugoton Field. And it was designed to correct correlative rights out there and that the price of gas in the Kansas Hugoton Field and the -- the conditions under which gas is produced in the Kansas Hugoton Field resulted from economic conditions peculiar to that field where a large number of small producers and landowners were facing drainage by reason of the monopolistic practices of the pipelines. The -- there was a destructive competition among the small producers in trying to secure these pipeline connections. And gas became a distress commodity and it was treated as a value -- a virtually valueless commodity. It was blown in to air. It was -- the wells were -- after the completion of the wells, they were -- they were blown for completion purposes carbon black plants sprang up in the area, manufacturing suit and using only about 92 or 9% or 10% of the energy of the gas. In -- in short, a particle use and waste of this irreplaceable natural resource came into being. And the evidence show that -- that there was a real relationship between treating this gas as a valueless or virtually valueless commodity and these wastes. The record shows how this happened. There were various wildcatters and the independents who first discovered the field. There were just a few pipelines in the area. It wasn't economically feasible to market this gas or to build a pipeline to market gas even from the largest wells or the 10 largest wells in the field. You can't carry gas in the bucket like cooking oil. You can't transport it from the truck. You can't put it on a rail. There's only one way to get it there and that's through pipelines. And these pipelines constituted monopolies out there and the small independents and the producers were captive suppliers. And they fought for connections and they lost some leases trying to get connections. It cost about $25 to $30 million to build a pipeline. And these pipeline companies, instead of giving the connections, and this is all in the record, instead of giving them connections, it would buy a lease right next to their leases and they drain their gas up from under them. And so these independent producers, they wouldn't buy their gas. They refuse to buy their gas. Well, I'll have to continue this --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Finish that in the morning.

turns.name: DALE M. STUCKY
turns.text: -- in the morning.
